speaker: interviewer
text: With Paula Tencer, tape three. You were talking about the establishment of Gestapo headquarters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES] And, we were-- we had a foreboding of tragedy with the Gestapo entering Brody. And my father was exhausted and worn out and he tried his best to give us hope. The Germans, the German Gestapo, after organizing a Judenrat, you call it, a group of-- it happened-- Jews who were educated and prominent, and through them, they were capable-- through them they made demands. They needed gold. They needed many things. But the first thing, what they did, is announced through Judenrat that now that the front is distant, that normal conditions will be restored. Useful Jews will be rewarded, professional people, doctors, lawyers, teachers-- maybe not so lawyers. But teachers, any professionals, will get jobs through them. There will be no other opportunity for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there is a meeting-- the Gestapo occupied-- it was a business school before the war. And they occupied that building. And there's a meeting, and people of profession, professional people, should report. And so there was some that were apprehended. They figure, we cannot trust the Germans. And some people said, how are we going to make a living if they say that if we are useful, we have to live with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some people went. The meeting went well. They treated them nicely, offered them cigarettes, called them herren und damen, ladies and gentlemen. And they told them that normal conditions will be restored. And specific details of salary, of rewards, they'll discuss at the next day, but just they wanted to bring it to their attention.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they came back, they said, the Germans were very polite, very friendly, very sympathetic. And they said that useful Jews will have it good. And they want-- they'll discuss more details tomorrow. So the professionals who were hesitant a day before went.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a blunder, because the gates were locked. There were trucks in the rear. And they forced them to climb in the trucks, prostrate themselves, and lay us one on top of the other. And when there was a sound made, the German Gestapo on top with the rifle hit him in the head. So even if he was in pain, they didn't dare to make a sound. And they loaded them up, layers of people on trucks, and went through town in broad daylight. Nobody heard a sound, because every time there was a sound, there was a beating. They brought him to outskirts of city, of the town. They already had-- there was-- our forest were a beautiful forest, which I described. To the left was a deposit of lime. And how cleverly that lime will prevent from spreading bacteria. It's the best way for burying bodies. They will decompose quicker. The trenches were dug, probably by farmers, peasants, paid. And they'll machine-gun them and throw them in and bury them and cover them with lime. The parents or wives of the men that went, when the evening was approaching and they did not see them return, they were concerned. And after more time elapsed, they went by the Gestapo. There was the Gestapo with a flag up there, insignia, frightening. And they said, don't worry, they'll come home. Don't worry.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the night passed. They did not come. The next day they did not come. And there was a cry in our town, like [? Yom ?] [? Kippur ?] when you cry, everybody knocking themselves with their hands over the head, why did I let them go? What happened? They did not know. They were driven away if they came near that structure there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then there came some people, some gentiles-- I don't know whether they were Ukrainians or Poles-- who said, we know where they took them. And they mentioned a town, a working camp, that they're working there. And a few days later, they came and they said, we spoke to your relative, and they said to give them some money and some clothes, because they're working there. They're detained. This was a lie. They robbed the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people waited. Where it is? Could we send packages? Where is it? Until there's always somebody who squeals, maybe perhaps those peasants who were doing the digging, that pointed out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we knew that they were killed. The whole intelligencia, they called them, intelligent people, they were killed. And then we were wiser people. We realized that the Germans are going to gradually destroy our lives. As I mentioned, we had to wear armbands. Four-- there was a curfew. 4:00 we cannot be in the streets. The Judenrat had established themselves in a certain quarters. And they found themselves in a position where somebody had to represent the Jews. I think it's a terrible thing for a Jew to make demands and follow orders for German to incriminate your people. But if you don't do it, they'll come directly and kill. So they were the tools in the Germans, in the hands of the Germans. The Germans made demands, so much gold by weight, to a deadline. If it's not delivered, there'll be a pogrom. At the beginning, if you come to a Jew who he thinks that maybe now with the Germans with a ring, with something, he could buy his life and buy his bread. He's hesitant of giving it. So he thinks, let somebody else give who's richer. They had a hard time collecting. And when they did not collect the amount that they expected, they took 50 men and shot them in the marketplace as a warning that if their demands are not met, that's what's going to happen. Naturally now, whoever had it, whatever you had, you gave to save lives. We didn't have much to give with my father because of our fire and so on. But whatever we had, we gave some. And some of it-- my father had friend, a Polish friend. It was a man, a couple. Kocian was their name, a lifetime friend. So we brought some things that were saved from the fire to them to hold it for us, that if a time comes when were are starving, where our life depends on buying a loaf of bread or something, that we should have something. Because if we have it with us, the Germans may barge in and take it away. We need some material things for our future safety. We don't know what tomorrow brings.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the killing of those people, everybody gave. When the time came and that demand was met, it was quiet for some time. After a certain time, they came with other demands. They even wanted thoroughbred horses, so men who for-- where does a Jew know about thoroughbred horses? So we had to find connections. There were some people who were very capable and who could organize. They did [? a little ?] [? bit. ?] Unbelievable demands that was given-- money, gold-- money, Polish money, was not much of value-- just valuables, gold, silver, to pacify them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my cousin [? Regina ?] came to our house, and she said that on the outskirt of town there is a Russian prison camp. Because as the Russians were retreating, they caught some Russians and kept them. There was a prison camp, barracks. And they're looking for girls to peel potatoes. And at the end of the day, they give us bread and marmalade and a ration.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was excited-- do you want to come? So I went. And we came-- we were able to-- after 4:00, we were not allowed, but in the daytime we were. So we went there. And there was sacks of potatoes. There was a German kitchen for the German personnel, kitchen for the prisoner, the barracks. They were under barbed wire.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, they load them on trucks and took them on highways-- there were many [? bombed-out ?] [INAUDIBLE] -- to work. And then under guard, they brought them back. And they were all-- some of them had sores. And they still had their uniforms. And we were peeling potatoes. And after a few days, I had sores from the knives, so I bandaged my fingers, peeling potatoes. And the German cook was always angry that we are not doing a good job, yelling at us. But we ate the soup the prisoners ate, which was-- they didn't even-- with some vegetables. They didn't even wash the vegetables. There were worms floating. But at the end of the day, we got bread, a small jar of marmalade, some margarine. And I felt good that I'm providing. I'm bringing home. [PAUSES] We saw-- the prisoners were like by the wire, standing around, hanging around in the yard. And when the German passed, when they smoked a cigarette, they just threw their butts down. They wanted that piece for their cigarette. So with a stick, they tried to reach for it, I saw. Scrape-- they almost got it. And finally they got it. They opened it up with a piece of newspaper. They spread that tobacco far. And when they lit it-- it was like a flame-- everybody had a smoke. [PAUSES] They were bringing them back. They lined them up by the kitchen to stand in line with their pack that they have to pour in some soup with worms. And near the kitchen there, there was like a garbage pile where the cook threw out the peels and garbage. So one of the soldiers, while in line, went over and picked some of this. They were hungry. They were starving. The cook saw it. With a big cane, he went over and started hitting him and hitting him-- he was lying down-- kicking and hitting and hitting him until he didn't move anymore. He was dead. They dragged him away. You saw the faces of the other soldiers. That is prison, and those were the Germans. This cook-- if he was a cook, he was not in the military, but he was cruel and hateful. It was a terrible experience for us to see. And we worked there for some time. And then, they were leaving, going forward, the-- the whole group. I don't know what they did with the prisoners, but they announced that they are moving on. They were moving it more towards Russia. I don't know if they killed the prisoners, if they bothered keeping them. So our job was finished. It was necessary-- they were catching now young men to work in the streets and houses, taking them out. Judenrat had to provide workers, because many highways were bombed. They had to rebuild them. So the Judenrat had to provide people. So they loaded people in trucks, and they're working. In our town, there was a group. They called them Organisation Todt. It was a private engineering company during the German occupation, during the war. They were militarized. And they were the ones that built bomb-- rebuilt bombed bridges in the front line and following with the army. And they were assigned to fix bridges and pour tar. If they have to retreat or go forward, the roads there should be smooth. And then they needed people for work. And they set up their offices and their quarters where the Jewish private school was where I went to school. And in the middle was a square yard, where during recess the children were playing. And above on a second floor, there were balconies going through that led to the ladies' room, to the men's room, for the teachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the yard, we could see them going. And I saw-- little young child, when I saw my teacher going, we had such respect. I was thinking, does she perform the same functions like us, a teacher? Does she do the same things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I remembered that courtyard and all that. That's-- the German quarters were there. And they were hiring people. I heard they need girls to clean and so. And I figure we'll have to. I went, and there was a man in charge. Trunzer-- Herman Trunzer was his name. He was aristocratic-looking, maybe in his late 50s. But he was an engineer. And now during the war, he wore the German uniform. He had some quarters in the private house. At the time, I did not know. He needs a girl to clean his room. So I got a job to work for Herr Trunzer. And in the afternoon when he goes home to rest from the office, somebody should bring him his dinner from the military kitchen, and he wears those high officers boots-- he has two pair-- so to polish his boots, layout his-- whatever has to be done. So I got the job. Later, I found out that they are organizing. They need men for work. They load them on trucks for work on the highways. So I asked Mr. Trunzer after I worked for him for a while if my two brothers could work, because if they work, they have a document that they are employed so they couldn't catch them to take them to far-away camps-- there were no concentration camps-- work camps. But they caught them. They had raids in catching young men.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they hired my two brothers though there. Mr. Trunzer saw to it that he works. And my brothers worked on a highway pouring tar and doing all those-- all those physical work. Their hands were-- they were not born to that and chopping the gravel and doing-- there was somebody in charge who knew how to do it, and they were supervised. And at the end of the day, they received some small rations of food. And they had-- the main thing was that they had documents that they were employed, so they were untouchable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where Mr. Trunzer lived, where he had his room, was in a private house, Mrs. Ethel Schubert. She was a very beautiful lady, Jewish lady, who occupied a beautiful house, the most elegant house in Brody, two-story house with balconies and many rooms. It was a two-story house. Downstairs was a different owner. She had the upstairs, going through a stairway to her apartment-- beautiful, beautiful view. Her husband, under Russian occupation, committed suicide because she was considered capitalist wealthy. To Brody's standard, he was rich. They had wholesale products, distributing to stores, and he was wealthy. And they were able to save some products in storage. And during Russian occupation, he was the capitalist, a [? candidate, ?] and his [INAUDIBLE] would have to be taken to be Siberia. And he was so afraid that they're going to come at night and take him to Siberia that he committed suicide. It came upon her to go down to the cellar and find him hung. He hung himself. Maybe he was-- I mean people go through all that, and they don't do it. He went into such depression and fear. And it came upon her to tell the children. But she was a very courageous woman. She occupied that house. And when the Germans came and they needed quarters, they wanted to evacuate her. She should remove-- move from her premises. And she begged them. She said, I'm going to clean your rooms and do all the chores for you. So let me stay just in one room and in the kitchen with my children, which was a good deal. So she stayed on in her house. And Mr. Trunzer occupied one of those rooms, beautiful rooms. So I came to work for him. So my chore was to clean his room. Mrs. Schubert was her name-- Ethel Schubert. She polished those parquet floors and did all the-- and did their laundry. But it was a whole upstairs, a whole floor. She could not have done it alone. So between the two of us, we did it. I did Mr. Trunzer's room. And when we were finished, I helped her. And we were working on those brushes strapped to our feet, where you could dance on it to polish that parquet floors. And I found in her a friend. She was a beautiful and a courageous woman. And Mr. Trunzer, I would go and fetch his dinner. And I was allowed also to get some soup from the kitchen to bring-- to rush home during slow time to bring to my parents. And I was so grateful that my brothers were employed. And also they brought home some supplies. And in the meantime, they were-- now they came out with-- they say aktion, they call it, roundups of Jews, at that time to camps. But those were not-- at that time, we did not know about death camps, about Auschwitz. They came to the Judenrat, made a demand-- so many, let's say 500, Jews, 1,000 Jews. When I tell you that we had 22,000, population, they brought in from the adjoining villages Jews, which very few Jews were farmers or something, but they collected them and brought them into the ghetto, though the ghetto was more congested. But at that time, there was no ghetto. And-- I lose my trains of thought.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about aktions, roundups.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, roundup of Jews. And so they came, made demands to the Judenrat to provide 1,000 Jews. That was a terrible function for them. But if they don't do it, the Ukrainians will do it. They told them that it's to work camps, not death camps at that time. Perhaps they did not have death camps. We did not know. So they went from house to house unexpectedly and caught Jews. Early at dawn, they had freight trains already ready in the station. And they would-- early in the morning, people didn't expect-- surround houses and barged in-- rouse, rouse, rouse, immediately out! Maybe they could get a few of their belongings in a hurry. Lined them up and then loaded them on trucks and to trains to camps. [PAUSES] They also had camps near our vicinity, working camps where they came out with a very clever idea. When they kept-- caught the young people, they made demands that if you pay some ransom, we'll release them. And that was through the Judenrat. If you pay, we'll let your son go home. So when-- even if you're poor, but if your son is caught and you're afraid they're going to kill him there, whenever you had, you gave them. And they let him home. They released him. But very shortly, they caught him to another camp. That was to reduce the people of their possessions and strip them of whatever they had. By then there were distance camps where they said they're working camps. Also, with some money, whoever had money could free them temporary. And then we found out about death camps. There was rumors, but we did not want to believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942, yes. And then we-- there was one aktion that they called-- they rounded up people-- when we knew already that they're taking them to concentration camps. Sometimes we were able, through Ukrainians, to hear that they had freight trains on the tracks. So that they're expecting that they're going to be a raid on Jews, a raid. So people would go to the farms and hide and pay Christians for hiding them-- if they knew in advance. Some had money that already went, like to hibernate in a place-- they had money and valuables-- that thought that they're going to live through the war. They did not know how the war will last. Sometimes we were caught. In the morning, we heard shooting. A raid is in process. And we have no place to run. We in the cellar had a place to run, that attic that my father build under the roof-- with a ladder we-- we-- heard in the morning shooting. And we knew what it was. So we all, shivering and frightened, we went up. But before that, we realized that we must get rid of our puppy. Because when we want to sit still, nobody should know that we exist there. The puppy is barking when he hears footsteps. So we gave-- a farmer that my father knew, we gave our puppy away.

speaker: interviewer
text: That puppy was the second Rex, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: That newly acquired-- yes, that was Rex the second, because our dog was killed. And very shortly, we acquired another dog, a puppy. And we had to get rid, even though we loved him. The farmer came and took the dog. A few days passed, and at night, we heard some scratch at the door. Our dog came back. He found his way back. Three times he came back. And the third time, the farmer chained him down, so he never came. We felt safe in that cellar during some roundups of Jews, because nobody knew that we lived there. But when there was a raid on Jews to take for death-- and we didn't know death camps, concentration camps-- my father had the idea we could hide in that roof that he built. It's like an attic that we could hide underneath.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we climbed up when we heard a shooting in the morning, without warning. And my father pulled up the ladder, and we were there. You couldn't stand up. You couldn't sit up, because it was not too high. So we had to lay there flat and wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard much shooting going on in town, frightened, not knowing what was going on. I remember my younger sister Rosa, she had a stomachache. But there was nothing that could be done, no place to go. She cannot even sit up. So she was suffering laying there. And that laying position, just like buried alive, we heard shots. We heard young boys running, obviously Christian boys, running in excitement. We heard shooting because when they were rounding up the Jews, some tried to run away. Some were sick in bed, invalids. They killed them. And they-- when they had the quota that they had-- let's say so many Jews that they had to have for that time to fill that train, that transport, there was a siren, like it's over. And people were able to come out. But we were afraid to come out. What's going-- we stayed there until dark. Then we lowered the ladder, and we came down. And when my mother's turn came to come down, she fainted because the position she was laying and all that. And when it was pitch dark, we had some friends that came at night, came to see us, and they give us a version of what took place. They told us who was taken away, who was shot.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's was a very depressing, gloomy perspective. What's going to happen? [INAUDIBLE] what's going to happen to us? Very much despair-- how long are we going to be able to hide and survive? But we found comfort in talking to each other. And the next day, we went to work, because once that they had their quota, it's quiet. My-- I remember my sister Laura, as we were sitting and talking, she was very realistic. She said, why are we waiting to be killed, sitting like sitting ducks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were some people that, after several raids on Jews, there was no illusion anymore. It's just a matter of time. They're going to get us. There were many families who had access to drugs who kill instantly, and whole families committed suicide. They just swallowed whatever pill, poison, and died. And my Laura was depressed. [PAUSES] And she said, we are-- we cannot live with this illusion we're just waiting, that expectation of that. We should kill ourselves. We should light the stove with coal, and then we shut the chimney so the carbon monoxide-- you go to sleep and you fall asleep. I cried out, you must not do that! You must not kill without my permission! Hitler may die tomorrow. Something will happen to stop. The world will not stand by and see these things taking place. They'll-- they'll come to our rescue. We must not kill ourselves. We must hope. She said, you foolish little dreamer. Live if you want to, but I wish never to wake up.
